title: Elucidating the Design Space of Diffusion-Based Generative Models [PDF]

## **Elucidating the Design Space of Diffusion-Based Generative Models**

**Tero Karras Miika Aittala** NVIDIA NVIDIA `tkarras@nvidia.com maittala@nvidia.com` **Timo Aila Samuli Laine** NVIDIA NVIDIA `taila@nvidia.com slaine@nvidia.com`

## **Abstract**

We argue that the theory and practice of diffusion-based generative models are currently unnecessarily convoluted and seek to remedy the situation by presenting a design space that clearly separates the concrete design choices. This lets us identify several changes to both the sampling and training processes, as well as preconditioning of the score networks. Together, our improvements yield new state-of-the-art FID of 1.79 for CIFAR-10 in a class-conditional setting and 1.97 in an unconditional setting, with much faster sampling (35 network evaluations per image) than prior designs. To further demonstrate their modular nature, we show that our design changes dramatically improve both the efficiency and quality obtainable with pre-trained score networks from previous work, including improving the FID of a previously trained ImageNet-64 model from 2.07 to near-SOTA 1.55, and after re-training with our proposed improvements to a new SOTA of 1.36.

## **1 Introduction**

Diffusion-based generative models [45] have emerged as a powerful new framework for neural image synthesis, in both unconditional [16, 36, 48] and conditional [17, 35, 36, 38, 39, 41, 42, 48] settings, even surpassing the quality of GANs [13] in certain situations [9]. They are also rapidly finding use in other domains such as audio [27, 37] and video [19] generation, image segmentation [4, 54] and language translation [34]. As such, there is great interest in applying these models and improving them further in terms of image/distribution quality, training cost, and generation speed.

The literature on these models is dense on theory, and derivations of sampling schedule, training dynamics, noise level parameterization, etc., tend to be based as directly as possible on theoretical frameworks, which ensures that the models are on a solid theoretical footing. However, this approach has a danger of obscuring the available design space — a proposed model may appear as a tightly coupled package where no individual component can be modified without breaking the entire system.

As our first contribution, we take a look at the theory behind these models from a practical standpoint, focusing more on the “tangible” objects and algorithms that appear in the training and sampling phases, and less on the statistical processes from which they might be derived. The goal is to obtain better insights into how these components are linked together and what degrees of freedom are available in the design of the overall system. We focus on the broad class of models where a neural network is used to model the score [22] of a noise level dependent marginal distribution of the training data corrupted by Gaussian noise. Thus, our work is in the context of _denoising score matching_ [51].

Our second set of contributions concerns the sampling processes used to synthesize images using diffusion models. We identify the best-performing time discretization for sampling, apply a higherorder Runge–Kutta method for the sampling process, evaluate different sampler schedules, and analyze the usefulness of stochasticity in the sampling process. The result of these improvements is a significant drop in the number of sampling steps required during synthesis, and the improved sampler can be used as a drop-in replacement with several widely used diffusions models [36, 48].

The third set of contributions focuses on the training of the score-modeling neural network. While we continue to rely on the commonly used network architectures (DDPM [16], NCSN [47]), we provide the first principled analysis of the preconditioning of the networks’ inputs, outputs, and loss functions in a diffusion model setting and derive best practices for improving the training dynamics. We also suggest an improved distribution of noise levels during training, and note that non-leaking augmentation [25] — typically used with GANs — is beneficial for diffusion models as well.

Taken together, our contributions enable significant improvements in result quality, e.g., leading to record FIDs of 1.79 for CIFAR-10 [28] and 1.36 for ImageNet [8] in 64 _×_ 64 resolution. With all key ingredients of the design space explicitly tabulated, we believe that our approach will allow easier innovation on the individual components, and thus enable more extensive and targeted exploration of the design space of diffusion models. Our implementation and pre-trained models are available at `https://github.com/NVlabs/edm`

## **2 Expressing diffusion models in a common framework**

Let us denote the data distribution by _p_ data( _**x**_ ), with standard deviation _σ_ data, and consider the family of mollified distributions _p_ ( _**x**_ ; _σ_ ) obtained by adding i.i.d. Gaussian noise of standard deviation _σ_ to the data. For _σ_ max _≫ σ_ data, _p_ ( _**x**_ ; _σ_ max) is practically indistinguishable from pure Gaussian noise. The idea of diffusion models is to randomly sample a noise image _**x**_ 0 _∼N_ ( **0** _, σ_ max[2] **[I]**[)][, and sequentially] denoise it into images _**x** i_ with noise levels _σ_ 0 = _σ_ max _> σ_ 1 _> · · · > σN_ = 0 so that at each noise level _**x** i ∼ p_ ( _**x** i_ ; _σi_ ). The endpoint _**x** N_ of this process is thus distributed according to the data.

Song et al. [48] present a stochastic differential equation (SDE) that maintains the desired distribution _p_ as sample _**x**_ evolves over time. This allows the above process to be implemented using a stochastic solver that both removes and adds noise at each iteration. They also give a corresponding “probability flow” ordinary differential equation (ODE) where the only source of randomness is the initial noise image _**x**_ 0. Contrary to the usual order of treatment, we begin by examining the ODE, as it offers a fruitful setting for analyzing sampling trajectories and their discretizations. The insights carry over to stochastic sampling, which we reintroduce as a generalization in Section 4.

**ODE formulation.** A probability flow ODE [48] continuously increases or reduces noise level of the image when moving forward or backward in time, respectively. To specify the ODE, we must first choose a schedule _σ_ ( _t_ ) that defines the desired noise level at time _t_ . For example, setting _σ_ ( _t_ ) _∝ √t_ is mathematically natural, as it corresponds to constant-speed heat diffusion [12]. However, we will show in Section 3 that the choice of schedule has major practical implications and should not be made on the basis of theoretical convenience.

The defining characteristic of the probability flow ODE is that evolving a sample _**x** a ∼ p_ � _**x** a_ ; _σ_ ( _ta_ )� from time _ta_ to _tb_ (either forward or backward in time) yields a sample _**x** b ∼ p_ � _**x** b_ ; _σ_ ( _tb_ )�. Intuitively, an infinitesimal forward step of this ODE nudges the sample away from the data, at a rate that depends on the change in noise level. Equivalently, a backward step nudges the sample towards the data distribution.

**Denoising score matching.** The score function has the remarkable property that it does not depend on the generally intractable normalization constant of the underlying density function _p_ ( _**x**_ ; _σ_ ) [22], and thus can be much easier to evaluate. where _**y**_ is a training image and _**n**_ is noise. In this light, the score function isolates the noise component from the signal in _**x**_ , and Eq. 1 amplifies (or diminishes) it over time. Figure 1 illustrates the behavior of ideal _D_ in practice. The key observation in diffusion models is that _D_ ( _**x**_ ; _σ_ ) can be implemented as a neural network _Dθ_ ( _**x**_ ; _σ_ ) trained according to Eq. 2. Note that _Dθ_ may include additional pre- and post-processing steps, such as scaling _**x**_ to an appropriate dynamic range; we will return to such _preconditioning_ in Section 5.

This requires choosing both the integration scheme (e.g., Euler or a variant of Runge–Kutta), as well as the discrete sampling times _{t_ 0 _, t_ 1 _, . . . , tN }_ . Many prior works rely on Euler’s method, but we show in Section 3 that a 2[nd] order solver offers a better computational tradeoff. For brevity, we do not provide a separate pseudocode for Euler’s method applied to our ODE here, but it can be extracted from Algorithm 1 by omitting lines 6–8.

**Putting it together.** Table 1 presents formulas for reproducing deterministic variants of three earlier methods in our framework. These methods were chosen because they are widely used and achieve state-of-the-art performance, but also because they were derived from different theoretical foundations. Some of our formulas appear quite different from the original papers as indirection and recursion have been removed; see Appendix C for details. The main purpose of this reframing is to bring into light all the independent components that often appear tangled together in previous work. In our framework, there are no implicit dependencies between the components — any choices (within reason) for the individual formulas will, in principle, lead to a functioning model. In other words, changing one component does not necessitate changes elsewhere in order to, e.g., maintain the property that the model converges to the data in the limit. In practice, some choices and combinations will of course work better than others.

## **3 Improvements to deterministic sampling**

Improving the output quality and/or decreasing the computational cost of sampling are common topics in diffusion model research (e.g., [10, 24, 30, 31, 32, 36, 43, 50, 52, 53, 56]). Our hypothesis is that the choices related to the sampling process are largely independent of the other components, such as network architecture and training details. In other words, the training procedure of _Dθ_ should not dictate _σ_ ( _t_ ), _s_ ( _t_ ), and _{ti}_ , nor vice versa; from the viewpoint of the sampler, _Dθ_ is simply a black box [52, 53]. We test this by evaluating different samplers on three _pre-trained_ models, each representing a different theoretical framework and model family. We first measure baseline results for these models using their original sampler implementations, and then bring these samplers into our unified framework using the formulas in Table 1, followed by our improvements. This allows us to evaluate different practical choices and propose general improvements to the sampling process that are applicable to all models.

We evaluate the “DDPM++ cont. (VP)” and “NCSN++ cont. (VE)” models by Song et al. [48] trained on unconditional CIFAR-10 [28] at 32 _×_ 32, corresponding to the variance preserving (VP) and variance exploding (VE) formulations [48], originally inspired by DDPM [16] and SMLD [47]. We also evaluate the “ADM (dropout)” model by Dhariwal and Nichol [9] trained on class-conditional ImageNet [8] at 64 _×_ 64, corresponding to the improved DDPM (iDDPM) formulation [36]. This model was trained using a discrete set of _M_ = 1000 noise levels. Further details are given in Appendix C.

We evaluate the result quality in terms of Fréchet inception distance (FID) [15] computed between 50,000 generated images and all available real images. Figure 2 shows FID as a function of neural function evaluations (NFE), i.e., how many times _Dθ_ is evaluated to produce a single image. Given that the sampling process is dominated entirely by the cost of _Dθ_ , improvements in NFE translate directly to sampling speed. The original deterministic samplers are shown in blue, and the reimplementations of these methods in our unified framework (orange) yield similar but consistently better results. The differences are explained by certain oversights in the original implementations as well as our more careful treatment of discrete noise levels in the case of DDIM; see Appendix C. Note that our reimplementations are fully specified by Algorithm 1 and Table 1, even though the original codebases are structured very differently from each other.

4

**Algorithm 1** Deterministic sampling using Heun’s 2[nd] order method with arbitrary _σ_ ( _t_ ) and _s_ ( _t_ ).

**Discretization and higher-order integrators.** Solving an ODE numerically is necessarily an approximation of following the true solution trajectory. At each step, the solver introduces _truncation error_ that accumulates over the course of _N_ steps. The local error generally scales superlinearly with respect to step size, and thus increasing _N_ improves the accuracy of the solution.

The commonly used Euler’s method is a first order ODE solver with _O_ ( _h_[2] ) local error with respect to step size _h_ . Higher-order Runge–Kutta methods [49] scale more favorably but require multiple evaluations of _Dθ_ per step. Linear multistep methods have also been recently proposed for sampling diffusion models [30, 56]. Through extensive tests, we have found Heun’s 2[nd] order method [2] (a.k.a. improved Euler, trapezoidal rule) — previously explored in the context of diffusion models by Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. [24] — to provide an excellent tradeoff between truncation error and NFE. As illustrated in Algorithm 1, it introduces an additional correction step for _**x** i_ +1 to account for change in d _**x** /_ d _t_ between _ti_ and _ti_ +1. This correction leads to _O_ ( _h_[3] ) local error at the cost of one additional evaluation of _Dθ_ per step. Note that stepping to _σ_ = 0 would result in a division by zero, so we revert to Euler’s method in this case. We discuss the general family of 2[nd] order solvers in Appendix D.2.

The time steps _{ti}_ determine how the step sizes and thus truncation errors are distributed between different noise levels. We provide a detailed analysis in Appendix D.1, concluding that the step size should decrease monotonically with decreasing _σ_ and it does not need to vary on a per-sample basis. We adopt a parameterized scheme where the time steps are defined according to a sequence of noise levels _{σi}_ , i.e., _ti_ = _σ[−]_[1] ( _σi_ ). We set _σi<N_ = ( _Ai_ + _B_ ) _[ρ]_ and select the constants _A_ and _B_ so that _σ_ 0 = _σ_ max and _σN −_ 1 = _σ_ min, which gives

Here _ρ_ controls how much the steps near _σ_ min are shortened at the expense of longer steps near _σ_ max. Our analysis in Appendix D.1 shows that setting _ρ_ = 3 nearly equalizes the truncation error at each step, but that _ρ_ in range of 5 to 10 performs much better for sampling images. This suggests that errors near _σ_ min have a large impact. We set _ρ_ = 7 for the remainder of this paper.

Results for Heun’s method and Eq. 5 are shown as the green curves in Figure 2. We observe consistent improvement in all cases: Heun’s method reaches the same FID as Euler’s method with considerably lower NFE.

**Trajectory curvature and noise schedule.** The shape of the ODE solution trajectories is defined by functions _σ_ ( _t_ ) and _s_ ( _t_ ). The choice of these functions offers a way to reduce the truncation errors discussed above, as their magnitude can be expected to scale proportional to the curvature of d _**x** /_ d _t_ . We argue that the best choice for these functions is _σ_ ( _t_ ) = _t_ and _s_ ( _t_ ) = 1, which is also the choice made in DDIM [46]. With this choice, the ODE of Eq. 4 simplifies to d _**x** /_ d _t_ = � _**x** − D_ ( _**x**_ ; _t_ )� _/t_ and _σ_ and _t_ become interchangeable.

An immediate consequence is that at any _**x**_ and _t_ , a single Euler step to _t_ = 0 yields the denoised image _Dθ_ ( _**x**_ ; _t_ ). The tangent of the solution trajectory therefore always points towards the denoiser output. This can be expected to change only slowly with the noise level, which corresponds to largely linear solution trajectories. The 1D ODE sketch of Figure 3c supports this intuition; the solution trajectories approach linear at both large and small noise levels, and have substantial curvature in only a small region in between. The effect of setting _σ_ ( _t_ ) = _t_ and _s_ ( _t_ ) = 1 is shown as the red curves in Figure 2. As DDIM already employs these same choices, the red curve is identical to the green one for ImageNet-64. However, VP and VE benefit considerably from switching away from their original schedules.

**Discussion.** The choices that we made in this section to improve deterministic sampling are summarized in the _Sampling_ part of Table 1. Together, they reduce the NFE needed to reach highquality results by a large factor: 7.3 _×_ for VP, 300 _×_ for VE, and 3.2 _×_ for DDIM, corresponding to the highlighted NFE values in Figure 2. In practice, we can generate 26.3 high-quality CIFAR-10 images per second on a single NVIDIA V100. The consistency of improvements corroborates our hypothesis that the sampling process is orthogonal to how each model was originally trained. As further validation, we show results for the adaptive RK45 method [11] using our schedule as the dashed black curves in Figure 2; the cost of this sophisticated ODE solver outweighs its benefits.

## **4 Stochastic sampling**

Deterministic sampling offers many benefits, e.g., the ability to turn real images into their corresponding latent representations by inverting the ODE. However, it tends to lead to worse output quality [46, 48] than stochastic sampling that injects fresh noise into the image in each step. Given that ODEs and SDEs recover the same distributions in theory, what exactly is the role of stochasticity?
**Background.** The SDEs of Song et al. [48] can be generalized [20, 55] as a sum of the probability flow ODE of Eq. 1 and a time-varying _Langevin diffusion_ SDE [14] (see Appendix B.5):

where _ωt_ is the standard Wiener process. d _**x**_ + and d _**x** −_ are now separate SDEs for moving forward and backward in time, related by the time reversal formula of Anderson [1]. The Langevin term can further be seen as a combination of a deterministic score-based denoising term and a stochastic noise injection term, whose net noise level contributions cancel out. As such, _β_ ( _t_ ) effectively expresses the relative rate at which existing noise is replaced with new noise. The SDEs of Song et al. [48] are recovered with the choice _β_ ( _t_ ) = _σ_ ˙ ( _t_ ) _/σ_ ( _t_ ), whereby the score vanishes from the forward SDE.

This perspective reveals why stochasticity is helpful in practice: The implicit Langevin diffusion drives the sample towards the desired marginal distribution at a given time, actively correcting for any errors made in earlier sampling steps. On the other hand, approximating the Langevin term

with discrete SDE solver steps introduces error in itself. Previous results [3, 24, 46, 48] suggest that non-zero _β_ ( _t_ ) is helpful, but as far as we can tell, the implicit choice for _β_ ( _t_ ) in Song et al. [48] enjoys no special properties. Hence, the optimal amount of stochasticity should be determined empirically.

**Our stochastic sampler.** We propose a stochastic sampler that combines our 2[nd] order deterministic ODE integrator with explicit Langevin-like “churn” of adding and removing noise. A pseudocode is given in Algorithm 2. At each step _i_ , given the sample _**x** i_ at noise level _ti_ (= _σ_ ( _ti_ )), we perform two sub-steps.ˆ First, we add noise to the sample according to a factor **ˆ** _γi ≥_ 0 to reach a higher noise level _ti_ = _ti_ + _γiti_ . Second, from the increased-noise sample _**x** i_ , we solve the ODE backward from ˆ _ti_ to _ti_ +1 with a single step. This yields a sample _**x** i_ +1 with noise level _ti_ +1, and the iteration continues. We stress that this is not a general-purpose SDE solver, but a sampling procedure tailored for the specific problem. Its correctness stems from the alternation of two sub-steps that each maintain the correct distribution (up to truncation error in the ODE step). The predictor-corrector sampler of Song et al. [48] has a conceptually similar structure to ours.

To analyze the main difference between our method and Euler–Maruyama, we first note a subtle discrepancy in the latter when discretizing Eq. 6. One can interpret Euler–Maruyama as first adding noise and then performing an ODE step, not from the intermediate state after noise injection, but assuming that _**x**_ and _σ_ remained at the initial state at the beginning of the iteration step. In our method, the parameters used to evaluate _Dθ_ on line 7 of Algorithm 2 correspond to the state after noise injection, whereas an Euler–Maruyama -like method would use _**x** i_ ; _ti_ instead of _**x**_ **ˆ** _i_ ; _t_[ˆ] _i_ . In the limit of ∆ _t_ approaching zero there may be no difference between these choices, but the distinction appears to become significant when pursuing low NFE with large steps.

**Practical considerations.** Increasing the amount of stochasticity is effective in correcting errors made by earlier sampling steps, but it has its own drawbacks. We have observed (see Appendix E.1) that excessive Langevin-like addition and removal of noise results in gradual loss of detail in the generated images with all datasets and denoiser networks. There is also a drift toward oversaturated colors at very low and high noise levels. We suspect that practical denoisers induce a slightly nonconservative vector field in Eq. 3, violating the premises of Langevin diffusion and causing these detrimental effects. Notably, our experiments with analytical denoisers (such as the one in Figure 1b) have not shown such degradation.

If the degradation is caused by flaws in _Dθ_ ( _**x**_ ; _σ_ ), they can only be remedied using heuristic means during sampling. We address the drift toward oversaturated colors by only enabling stochasticity within a specific range of noise levels _ti ∈_ [ _S_ tmin _, S_ tmax]. For these noise levels, we define _γi_ = _S_ churn _/N_ , where _S_ churn controls the overall amount of stochasticity. We further clamp _γi_ to never introduce more new noise than what is already present in the image. Finally, we have found that the loss of detail can be partially counteracted by setting _S_ noise slightly above 1 to inflate the standard deviation for the newly added noise. This suggests that a major component of the hypothesized non-conservativity of _Dθ_ ( _**x**_ ; _σ_ ) is a tendency to remove slightly too much noise — most likely due to regression toward the mean that can be expected to happen with any _L_ 2-trained denoiser [29].

**Evaluation.** Figure 4 shows that our stochastic sampler outperforms previous samplers [24, 36, 48] by a significant margin, especially at low step counts. Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. [24] use a standard

While our results showcase the potential gains achievable through sampler improvements, they also highlight the main shortcoming of stochasticity: For best results, one must make several heuristic choices — either implicit or explicit — that depend on the specific model. Indeed, we had to find the optimal values of _{S_ churn _, S_ tmin _, S_ tmax _, S_ noise _}_ on a case-by-case basis using grid search (Appendix E.2). This raises a general concern that using stochastic sampling as the primary means of evaluating model improvements may inadvertently end up influencing the design choices related to model architecture and training.

## **5 Preconditioning and training**

There are various known good practices for training neural networks in a supervised fashion. For example, it is advisable to keep input and output signal magnitudes fixed to, e.g., unit variance, and to avoid large variation in gradient magnitudes on a per-sample basis [5, 21]. Training a neural network to model _D_ directly would be far from ideal — for example, as the input _**x**_ = _**y**_ + _**n**_ is a combination of clean signal _**y**_ and noise _**n** ∼N_ ( **0** _, σ_[2] **I** ), its magnitude varies immensely depending on noise level _σ_ . For this reason, the common practice is to not represent _Dθ_ as a neural network directly, but instead train a different network _Fθ_ from which _Dθ_ is derived.

Previous methods [36, 46, 48] address the input scaling via a _σ_ -dependent normalization factor and attempt to precondition the output by training _Fθ_ to predict _**n**_ scaled to unit variance, from which the signal is then reconstructed via _Dθ_ ( _**x**_ ; _σ_ ) = _**x** − σFθ_ ( _·_ ). This has the drawback that at large _σ_ , the network needs to fine-tune its output carefully to cancel out the existing noise _**n**_ exactly and give the output at the correct scale; note that any errors made by the network are amplified by a factor of _σ_ . In this situation, it would seem much easier to predict the expected output _D_ ( _**x**_ ; _σ_ ) directly. In the same spirit as previous parameterizations that adaptively mix signal and noise (e.g., [10, 43, 50]), we propose to precondition the neural network with a _σ_ -dependent skip connection that allows it to estimate either _**y**_ or _**n**_ , or something in between. This form reveals the effective training target of _Fθ_ , allowing us to determine suitable choices for the preconditioning functions from first principles. As detailed in Appendix B.6, we derive our choices shown in Table 1 by requiring network inputs and training targets to have unit variance ( _c_ in, _c_ out), and amplifying errors in _Fθ_ as little as possible ( _c_ skip). The formula for _c_ noise is chosen empirically.

Table 2 shows FID for a series of training setups, evaluated using our deterministic sampler from Section 3. We start with the baseline training setup of Song et al. [48], which differs considerably between the VP and VE cases; we provide separate results for each (config A). To obtain a more meaningful point of comparison, we re-adjust the basic hyperparameters (config B) and improve the expressive power of the model (config C) by removing the lowest-resolution layers and doubling the capacity of the highest-resolution layers instead; see Appendix F.3 for further details. We then replace the original choices of _{c_ in _, c_ out _, c_ noise _, c_ skip _}_ with our preconditioning (config D), which keeps the results largely unchanged — except for VE that improves considerably at 64 _×_ 64 resolution. Instead of improving FID per se, the main benefit of our preconditioning is that it makes the training more robust, enabling us to turn our focus on redesigning the loss function without adverse effects.

**Loss weighting and sampling.** Eq. 8 shows that training _Fθ_ as preconditioned in Eq. 7 incurs an effective per-sample loss weight of _λ_ ( _σ_ ) _c_ out( _σ_ )[2] . To balance the effective loss weights, we set _λ_ ( _σ_ ) = 1 _/c_ out( _σ_ )[2] , which also equalizes the initial training loss over the entire _σ_ range as shown in Figure 5a (green curve). Finally, we need to select _p_ train( _σ_ ), i.e., how to choose noise levels during training. Inspecting the per- _σ_ loss after training (blue and orange curves) reveals that a significant reduction is possible only at intermediate noise levels; at very low levels, it is both difficult and irrelevant to discern the vanishingly small noise component, whereas at high levels the training targets are always dissimilar from the correct answer that approaches dataset average. Therefore, we target the training efforts to the relevant range using a simple log-normal distribution for _p_ train( _σ_ ) as detailed in Table 1 and illustrated in Figure 5a (red curve).

Table 2 shows that our proposed _p_ train and _λ_ (config E) lead to a dramatic improvement in FID in all cases when used in conjunction with our preconditioning (config D). In concurrent work, Choi et al. [6] propose a similar scheme to prioritize noise levels that are most relevant w.r.t. forming the perceptually recognizable content of the image. However, they only consider the choice of _λ_ in isolation, which results in a smaller overall improvement.

**Augmentation regularization.** To prevent potential overfitting that often plagues diffusion models with smaller datasets, we borrow an augmentation pipeline from the GAN literature [25]. The pipeline consists of various geometric transformations (see Appendix F.2) that we apply to a training image prior to adding noise. To prevent the augmentations from leaking to the generated images, we provide the augmentation parameters as a conditioning input to _Fθ_ ; during inference we set the them to zero to guarantee that only non-augmented images are generated. Table 2 shows that data augmentation provides a consistent improvement (config F) that yields new state-of-the-art FIDs of 1.79 and 1.97 for conditional and unconditional CIFAR-10, beating the previous records of 1.85 [44] and 2.10 [50].

**Stochastic sampling revisited.** Interestingly, the relevance of stochastic sampling appears to diminish as the model itself improves, as shown in Figure 5b,c. When using our training setup in CIFAR-10 (Figure 5b), the best results were obtained with deterministic sampling, and any amount of stochastic sampling was detrimental. **ImageNet-64.** As a final experiment, we trained a class-conditional ImageNet-64 model from scratch using our proposed training improvements. This model achieved a new state-of-the-art FID of 1.36 compared to the previous record of 1.48 [17]. We used the ADM architecture [9] with no changes, and trained it using our config E with minimal tuning; see Appendix F.3 for details. We did not find overfitting to be a concern, and thus chose to not employ augmentation regularization. As shown in Figure 5c, the optimal amount of stochastic sampling was much lower than with the pre-trained model, but unlike with CIFAR-10, stochastic sampling was clearly better than deterministic sampling. This suggests that more diverse datasets continue to benefit from stochastic sampling.

## **6 Conclusions**

Our approach of putting diffusion models to a common framework exposes a modular design. This allows a targeted investigation of individual components, potentially helping to better cover the viable design space. In our tests this let us simply replace the samplers in various earlier models, drastically improving the results. For example, in ImageNet-64 our sampler turned an average model (FID 2.07) to a challenger (1.55) for the previous SOTA model (1.48) [17], and with training improvements achieved SOTA FID of 1.36. We also obtained new state-of-the-art results on CIFAR-10 while using only 35 model evaluations, deterministic sampling, and a small network. The current high-resolution diffusion models rely either on separate super-resolution steps [17, 35, 39], subspace projection [23], very large networks [9, 48], or hybrid approaches [38, 41, 50] — we believe that our contributions are orthogonal to these extensions. That said, many of our parameter values may need to be re-adjusted for higher resolution datasets. Furthermore, we feel that the precise interaction between stochastic sampling and the training objective remains an interesting question for future work.

**Societal impact.** Our advances in sample quality can potentially amplify negative societal effects _·_ when used in a large-scale system like DALL E 2, including types of disinformation or emphasizing sterotypes and harmful biases [33]. The training and sampling of diffusion models needs a lot of electricity; our project consumed _∼_ 250MWh on an in-house cluster of NVIDIA V100s.

**Acknowledgments.** We thank Jaakko Lehtinen, Ming-Yu Liu, Tuomas Kynkäänniemi, Axel Sauer, Arash Vahdat, and Janne Hellsten for discussions and comments, and Tero Kuosmanen, Samuel Klenberg, and Janne Hellsten for maintaining our compute infrastructure.

progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper (commonly known as the **EDM** paper) is an exceptionally high-quality contribution that provides a much-needed "engineering-first" decomposition of the diffusion model pipeline. By decoupling the generative process into independent components—sampling schedules, ODE solvers, and network preconditioning—the authors successfully move away from the often opaque SDE derivations that characterized early literature. The clarity of the writing is excellent, and the introduction of a "common framework" serves as a significant pedagogical and structural improvement for the field. 

The paper's most significant strengths lie in its empirical rigor and the modularity of its findings, particularly the demonstration that their sampling and preconditioning improvements can be retrofitted to pre-trained models from other research groups. The identification of optimal time-discretization and higher-order solvers represents a major leap in sampling efficiency. However, a potential issue—though mitigated by the paper's scope—is the increased complexity introduced by the numerous hyperparameters involved in the "design space" (e.g., noise schedules, scaling factors, and solver parameters). While the paper provides robust defaults and principled derivations for these, the sheer density of the proposed modifications might make it challenging for practitioners to identify which single change provides the most marginal utility without adopting the entire EDM framework. Overall, this work sets a new standard for SOTA benchmarks and provides a definitive blueprint for next-generation diffusion research.

comments.explanation: The text states that $\hat{t}_i = t_i + \gamma_i t_i$. In Algorithm 2, line 5 defines $\hat{t}_i$ as a temporarily increased noise level. However, the subscript $i$ in $\gamma_i$ and $\hat{t}_i$ Suggests these are indexed to the current step. The explanation in line 5 of the algorithm table uses $t_i$ and the condition $t_i \in [S_{t\text{min}}, S_{t\text{max}}]$. Because $t_N=0$, the final steps of the loop will involve very small noise levels. If $\gamma_i$ is 0 outside a specific range, the sampler effectively reverts to the deterministic ODE. While this is likely an intentional design choice for Langevin dynamics (which are often disabled at very high/low noise), the thresholding logic is slightly disconnected from the ODE solver's requirement to reach exactly $t_{i+1}$. If $\gamma_i$ is large, the ODE solver is taking a much larger jump ($\hat{t}_i \to t_{i+1}$) than the intended discretization schedule ($t_i \to t_{i+1}$), potentially increasing truncation error in a way not accounted for in the $\rho=7$ analysis.

comments.title: Potential Indexing Error in Algorithm 2 2nd-order Correction
comments.quote: $\mathbf{x}_{i+1} \leftarrow \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i + (t_{i+1} - \hat{t}_i) \left( \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{d}_i + \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{d}'_i \right)$
comments.explanation: In line 11 of Algorithm 2, the second-order update uses $(t_{i+1} - \hat{t}_i)$ as the step size. This is consistent with the 'predictor' step in line 8. However, in line 10, the derivative $\mathbf{d}'_i$ is calculated using $\mathbf{x}_{i+1}$ and $t_{i+1}$. In standard 2nd-order Runge-Kutta (Heun), the weights and coordinates are straightforward. But here, because noise was injected to reach $\hat{t}_i$, the 'trapezoidal' average is being taken between a point that has extra stochastic noise ($\hat{\mathbf{x}}_i$) and a predicted point ($\mathbf{x}_{i+1}$). While the authors argue this is 'tailored for the specific problem,' the text does not fully justify why taking the average of derivatives $\mathbf{d}_i$ (at noise level $\hat{t}_i$) and $\mathbf{d}'_i$ (at $t_{i+1}$) is valid when the drift $\mathbf{d}_i$ was computed on a sample $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_i$ that does not lie on the original ODE trajectory of $\mathbf{x}_i$. It essentially assumes the score function's local geometry is invariant to the small Langevin perturbation, which is a significant implicit assumption for a 'principled' framework.

comments.title: Scale factor notation inconsistency in generalized stochastic sampling
comments.quote: \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i = \mathbf{x}_i + \sqrt{\hat{t}_i^2 - t_i^2} \epsilon_i
comments.explanation: In the Paper Summary (Key Equations) and Section 3, the authors argue that the best choice for the scaling factor is $s(t)=1$ and $\sigma(t)=t$. In this specialized case, the noise injection formula $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_i = \mathbf{x}_i + \sqrt{\hat{t}_i^2 - t_i^2} \epsilon_i$ is correct. However, Equation 4 and Table 1 explicitly define a generalized framework where $s(t)$ and $\sigma(t)$ can be arbitrary (e.g., the VP schedule). If $s(t) \neq 1$, the noise magnitude at time $t$ is actually $s(t)\sigma(t)$. The stochastic update formula provided in the summary lacks the $s(t)$ scaling necessary to remain consistent with the 'Generalized ODE' (Eq 4). While Section 3 justifies why $s(t)=1$ is preferred, the summary presents this as a 'Generalized SDE' update, which it is not; it is specific to the $s(t)=1$ case.

comments.title: Unjustified claim regarding NFE efficiency over adaptive solvers
comments.quote: It is an open question if adaptive solvers can be a net win over a well-tuned fixed schedule in sampling diffusion models.
comments.explanation: The authors state it is an 'open question' whether adaptive solvers are more efficient. However, in the 'Evaluation' paragraph of Section 4, they acknowledge that Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. [24] use a standard higher-order adaptive SDE solver, and the authors' own Figure 4 shows their fixed-step stochastic sampler significantly outperforming that baseline. By their own empirical evidence in Figure 4, they have provided a strong indication that for this domain, tuned fixed schedules (Heun) outperform at least one prominent adaptive implementation. Calling it an 'open question' after presenting data that suggests a clear winner feels unnecessarily cautious or contradictory to the visual evidence provided in the plots.

comments.title: Potential numerical instability in Euler step at boundary
comments.quote: \mathbf{d}_i \leftarrow \left[ \frac{\dot{\sigma}(t_i)}{\sigma(t_i)} + \frac{\dot{s}(t_i)}{s(t_i)} \right] \mathbf{x}_i - \frac{\dot{\sigma}(t_i)s(t_i)}{\sigma(t_i)} D_\theta \left( \frac{\mathbf{x}_i}{s(t_i)}; \sigma(t_i) \right)
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, line 4, the calculation of the derivative $\mathbf{d}_i$ involves dividing by $\sigma(t_i)$. While the text correctly notes in line 6 that one should not take a 2nd order step when $\sigma \to 0$, $t_N$ is defined in Table 1 as the point where $\sigma(t_N) = 0$. In many schedules (like VE), $\sigma(t)$ reaches very small values $\sigma_{min}$ before the final step. If a user follows the logic of the algorithm for the very last step $(i = N-1)$, and the schedule involves $\sigma(t_{N}) = 0$, the $x/s(t)$ and the multiplicative terms will be well-defined at $t_{N-1}$ but the algorithm needs to be extremely careful about the definition of $\dot{\sigma}/\sigma$ as $t \to 0$. In the 'Simplified ODE' in the summary, this is shown as $(\mathbf{x}-D)/t$. If $t_N=0$ is reached, the solver relies on a final Euler step. However, the calculation of $\mathbf{d}_{N-1}$ still requires $\sigma(t_{N-1})$. The paper implies $t_N=0$ is a hard limit, but does not explicitly state how to handle the singularity in the score function derivative $\mathbf{d}_i$ if $\sigma$ is allowed to be exactly 0 at the start of a step. Context suggests they stop at a very small $\sigma_{min}$, but Table 1 says $t_N=0$, creating a slight ambiguity for implementation.

progressive_original__gemini-3-flash-pre.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3-flash-pre.overall_feedback:
This paper (commonly known as the **EDM** paper) is an exceptionally high-quality contribution that provides a much-needed "engineering-first" decomposition of the diffusion model pipeline. By decoupling the generative process into independent components—sampling schedules, ODE solvers, and network preconditioning—the authors successfully move away from the often opaque SDE derivations that characterized early literature. The clarity of the writing is excellent, and the introduction of a "common framework" serves as a significant pedagogical and structural improvement for the field. 

The paper's most significant strengths lie in its empirical rigor and the modularity of its findings, particularly the demonstration that their sampling and preconditioning improvements can be retrofitted to pre-trained models from other research groups. The identification of optimal time-discretization and higher-order solvers represents a major leap in sampling efficiency. However, a potential issue—though mitigated by the paper's scope—is the increased complexity introduced by the numerous hyperparameters involved in the "design space" (e.g., noise schedules, scaling factors, and solver parameters). While the paper provides robust defaults and principled derivations for these, the sheer density of the proposed modifications might make it challenging for practitioners to identify which single change provides the most marginal utility without adopting the entire EDM framework. Overall, this work sets a new standard for SOTA benchmarks and provides a definitive blueprint for next-generation diffusion research.

The intended expression is clearly $\mathbf{x}_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \sigma_{\text{max}}^2 \mathbf{I})$. While a reader familiar with diffusion models can infer the meaning, the bracketed segments '[2] **[I]**[)][, and sequentially]' disrupt the formal definition of the starting distribution, which is a foundational component of the framework. This makes it impossible to verify the exact form of the ODE being analyzed in Section 2 without external knowledge of the cited paper [48].

comments.title: Unclear variable/constant C1 in iDDPM time steps
comments.explanation: In Table 1, under the 'Time steps' row for the iDDPM column, there is a reference to a constant $C_1$. Looking at the 'Parameters' section at the bottom of Table 1, $C_1$ is defined as 0.001. However, in the standard formulation of iDDPM (Improved Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models), the transition between noise levels usually follows a specific schedule (like cosine or linear). The introduction of $C_1$ as a clipping value for the ratio of alpha-bars is a specific implementation detail used to prevent division by zero or numerical instability in the ODE conversion, but its origin is not explicitly discussed in the text, potentially leading to confusion for a reader trying to map this directly to the original iDDPM paper [36].

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'M' and 'u_j' notation for pre-trained models
comments.explanation: Table 1 defines the time steps for iDDPM using a flooring function involving $j_0$, $M$, $N$, and $i$. This is intended to map the continuous sampler's steps onto the discrete set of noise levels the model was trained with. While the parameters $M=1000$ and $j_0=8$ are provided, the formula as rendered via OCR is extremely difficult to parse (e.g., the indices and fractions are jumbled). Given that this mapping is critical for using the 'improved sampler' as a 'drop-in replacement' for fixed-step models, the notation failure here makes the method difficult to reproduce for that specific model class.

comments.title: Possible sign error in simplified probability flow ODE
comments.quote: $\frac{d}{dt} \mathbf{x} = \left( \frac{\dot{\sigma}(t)}{\sigma(t)} + \frac{\dot{s}(t)}{s(t)} \right) \mathbf{x} - \frac{\dot{\sigma}(t)s(t)}{\sigma(t)} D(\mathbf{x}/s(t); \sigma(t))$
comments.explanation: In Section 2 under 'Time-dependent signal scaling', the authors presented a generalized ODE. Let's check the derivation for the standard case ($s(t)=1$). Equation 3 defines the score as $\nabla \log p = (D - \mathbf{x})/\sigma^2$. The standard Probability Flow ODE (per Song et al. 2020) is $d\mathbf{x} = -\frac{1}{2}\dot{\sigma^2} \nabla \log p \, dt$. Substituting the score gives $d\mathbf{x} = -\sigma \dot{\sigma} \frac{D - \mathbf{x}}{\sigma^2} dt = \dot{\sigma} \frac{\mathbf{x} - D}{\sigma} dt$. This simplifies to $\frac{\dot{\sigma}}{\sigma}\mathbf{x} - \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{\sigma}D$. The text formula matches this if $s(t)=1$ and $\dot{s}(t)=0$. However, checking against the OCR'd Table 1 and the intent of 'undoing scaling', the term involving $D$ typically requires the score to be evaluated at the correctly scaled $\mathbf{x}$. While the logic seems sound, the minus sign in the transition from Equation 1 (which 'nudges away' or 'towards' data) to the time-derivative formula is highly sensitive to whether $t$ is defined as increasing or decreasing noise. In the context of Algorithm 1, $t$ is decreasing. If $t$ decreases, $\dot{\sigma}$ is negative, which might flip the perceived direction. However, as written, the formula is consistent with the 'tangent points to denoiser' claim in Section 3. The risk here is a potential sign mismatch with standard SDE literature conventions, but it is internally consistent with the paper’s unique 'ODE-first' framework.

comments.title: Discrepancy in Noise Conditioning formula for iDDPM
comments.quote: $c_{\text{noise}}(\sigma) = M - 1 - \text{arg min}_j |u_j - \sigma|$
comments.explanation: In Table 1, for the iDDPM/ADM model, $c_{\text{noise}}(\sigma)$ is defined using an 'arg min' over a discrete set of training noise levels $u_j$. The text mentions that iDDPM was trained on a discrete set of $M=1000$ noise levels. However, in the deterministic sampling section, the paper advocates for a continuous ODE treatment. By using an 'arg min' (nearest neighbor) to find the noise index for a continuous $\sigma$, the authors introduce a piece-wise constant input to the neural network $F_\theta$. This creates 'jumps' in the ODE vector field at the boundaries between $u_j$ levels. This contradicts the stated goal of using a 2nd-order Heun solver, which assumes a smooth vector field to achieve $O(h^3)$ local error. While the authors admit they 're-trained' or 're-implemented' these, using a non-differentiable noise conditioning in an ODE solver is a technical inconsistency that might degrade the benefits of higher-order integration for that specific model.

comments.title: Potential OCR error in iDDPM time step formula
comments.quote: $u_{\lfloor j_0 + \frac{M-1-j_0}{N-1} (i + \lfloor \dots \rfloor) \rfloor}$
comments.explanation: Looking at the 'Time steps' row for 'iDDPM' in Table 1, the OCR has produced a very complex nested subscript: $u_{\lfloor j_0 + \dots \rfloor}$. Given the context of the paper (simplifying diffusion), and the footnote symbol $\dagger$ indicating $j_0=8$ was better than $j_0=0$, it appears the authors are mapping the $N$ sampling steps onto the original $M$ training indices. However, the presence of $\lfloor \dots \rfloor$ inside the index of $u$ suggests a nearest-neighbor mapping. If this is an OCR misread of a linear interpolation or a simpler sequence, it’s fine. But if the intended formula truly uses floor functions to index into a discrete set of noise levels while simultaneously claiming to use a 2nd-order Heun solver (which requires sampling the ODE at mid-points or end-points), the method is underspecified. A 2nd-order method requires evaluating the derivative at $t_{i+1}$, but if the noise conditioning $c_{noise}$ is pinned to the nearest $u_j$ via floor/argmin, the 'second order' correction may be calculating the same gradient as the first step if both $t_i$ and $t_{i+1}$ map to the same discrete index $j$.

comments.quote: $$d_i \leftarrow \left( \frac{\dot{\sigma}(t_i)}{\sigma(t_i)} + \frac{\dot{s}(t_i)}{s(t_i)} \right) \mathbf{x}_i - \frac{\dot{\sigma}(t_i)s(t_i)}{\sigma(t_i)} D_\theta \left( \frac{\mathbf{x}_i}{s(t_i)}; \sigma(t_i) \right)$$
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 explicitly handles the case where $\sigma(t_{i+1}) = 0$ in the correction step (Line 6), but Line 4 calculates $d_i$ using $\sigma(t_i)$ in the denominator. In most diffusion schedules (like VP or the proposed EDM schedule), the starting noise $\sigma(t_0) = \sigma_{max}$ is large, but the sampling progresses toward $\sigma=0$. If a user follows a schedule where $\sigma(t_N) = 0$, the loop in Line 3 processes $i$ up to $N-1$. If $t_{N-1}$ corresponds to a very small noise level or if the schedule reached zero at that point, the division by $\sigma(t_i)$ becomes numerically unstable. While Line 6 prevents the *correction* at zero, the primary Euler slope $d_i$ at the final step might still be problematic if the time step $t_i$ approaches the singularity at $\sigma=0$.

comments.quote: Second, from the increased-noise sample $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_i$, we solve the ODE backward from $\hat{t}_i$ to $t_{i+1}$ with a single step.
comments.explanation: In Section 4, the text describes a two-step process: adding noise to reach $\hat{t}_i$, then taking an 'ODE backward' step to $t_{i+1}$. Comparing this to Algorithm 1 (line 6), which says `if \sigma(t_{i+1}) = 0 then` [skip correction], Algorithm 2 seems to do the opposite: it only applies the 2nd-order correction when the target noise level is zero. If $t_{i+1}=0$ implies a first-order step (as argued in Section 3 to avoid division by zero), the logic in Alg 2 line 9 is inverted relative to Alg 1 line 6. This makes the stochastic sampler's exact procedure ambiguous for reproduction.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper is exemplary in its methodological rigor and clarity, demonstrating a high degree of technical maturity typical of the authors' work. Its most significant strength lies in transitioning the study of diffusion models from an often opaque "black-box" of theoretical derivations to a transparent, modular "design space." By decoupling the various components—such as noise schedules, preconditioning strategies, and ODE solver configurations—the authors provide a much-needed service to the field, making the architecture highly interpretable and accessible for future innovation. The structural flow is logical, moving seamlessly from a critique of existing literature to the proposal of a unified framework, and the empirical results (e.g., SOTA FID scores and increased sampling efficiency) provide compelling, objective validation of their design principles. The work avoids the common pitfall of overly complex formalism, instead focusing on "tangible" algorithmic components, which ensures the contributions are both theoretically sound and immediately actionable for researchers and practitioners alike.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: The paper is exemplary in its methodological rigor and clarity, demonstrating a high degree of technical maturity typical of the authors' work. Its most significant strength lies in transitioning the study of diffusion models from an often opaque "black-box" of theoretical derivations to a transparent, modular "design space." By decoupling the various components—such as noise schedules, preconditioning strategies, and ODE solver configurations—the authors provide a much-needed service to the field, making the architecture highly interpretable and accessible for future innovation. The structural flow is logical, moving seamlessly from a critique of existing literature to the proposal of a unified framework, and the empirical results (e.g., SOTA FID scores and increased sampling efficiency) provide compelling, objective validation of their design principles. The work avoids the common pitfall of overly complex formalism, instead focusing on "tangible" algorithmic components, which ensures the contributions are both theoretically sound and immediately actionable for researchers and practitioners alike.

Using $x_0$ for the noise image and $x_N$ (which equals $0$ for noise level, but signifies the end of the sequence) for the denoised image is mathematically confusing given the standard definition of the diffusion process where $x_0$ is the clean sample.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling and impactful contribution to the diffusion model literature, offering a much-needed pragmatic re-examination of the design space. Its quality is high, evidenced by the clear articulation of contributions and the subsequent state-of-the-art results. The writing is generally clear and logically structured, effectively separating the analysis of sampling, training, and preconditioning. However, the paper assumes significant prior familiarity with diffusion modeling literature (e.g., DDPM, NCSN, score matching), and the dense notation in Section 2 may be daunting for readers less steeped in the field. A minor clarity issue is that the introduction's enumeration of contributions, while thorough, feels slightly verbose and could be streamlined to improve focus. Most significantly, while the paper's strength is its practical, design-oriented approach, it occasionally downplays the theoretical underpinnings it seeks to simplify, which might leave theoretically inclined readers wanting more rigorous justification for certain heuristic choices (e.g., the noise level distribution during training). Overall, this is an excellent paper that will be highly influential, but its accessibility to a broader audience could be improved by providing more intuitive explanations alongside the technical details.

comments.title: Potential ambiguity in 'non-leaking augmentation' claim
comments.quote: We also suggest an improved distribution of noise levels during training, and note that non-leaking augmentation [25] — typically used with GANs — is beneficial for diffusion models as well.
comments.explanation: The text states that 'non-leaking augmentation' is beneficial for diffusion models. comments.title: Missing factor of 1/2 in the ODE formulation for the score function substitution
comments.explanation: The text combines equations (2) and (3) into a single line, stating that the score function equals (D(x;σ) - x)/σ^2. This is a standard result from denoising score matching (Vincent, 2011). However, the derivation from the minimization of the L2 denoising error in Eq. 2 typically yields ∇_x log p(x;σ) = (D(x;σ) - x)/σ^2 only if D is the optimal denoiser. The text correctly states this relationship. A careful reader might initially be concerned that the factor 1/2 from the derivative of the quadratic loss is missing, but it cancels out: minimizing E[||D - y||^2] w.r.t. D gives D = E[y|x] = x + σ^2 ∇_x log p(x;σ), hence ∇_x log p(x;σ) = (D - x)/σ^2. The bracketing notation ' ... ' and the combined equation numbering (2,3) are unconventional but not erroneous. No issue remains after checking the standard derivation.

comments.title: Parameter ρ=7 chosen but justification is heuristic
comments.quote: Our analysis in Appendix D.1 shows that setting ρ = 3 nearly equalizes the truncation error at each step, but that ρ in range of 5 to 10 performs much better for sampling images. This suggests that errors near σ_min have a large impact. We set ρ = 7 for the remainder of this paper.
comments.explanation: The authors state that ρ=3 equalizes truncation error but that ρ=5-10 performs better, then pick ρ=7 as a specific value. The reasoning that 'errors near σ_min have a large impact' is plausible but the choice of 7 within the 5-10 range appears somewhat arbitrary. No ablation study or quantitative comparison of different ρ values within the 5-10 range is presented in the provided text. While this is a standard hyperparameter choice and the paper likely has more details elsewhere, the justification in this passage is insufficient to explain why 7 specifically was chosen over, say, 6 or 8.

comments.title: Training loss uses ideal denoiser D instead of network Dθ
comments.quote: Taking a weighted expectation of Eq. 2 over the noise levels gives the overall training loss E _σ,_ _**y** ,_ _**n**_  _λ_ ( _σ_ ) _∥D_ ( _**y**_ + _**n**_ ; _σ_ ) _−_ _**y** ∥_ 2[2]
comments.explanation: Equation 2 in the paper defines D(·;σ) as the optimal (ideal) denoiser that minimizes the expected L2 error for a given noise level. The actual training loss for the neural network should involve Dθ, not D. The text immediately following discusses the effective target for Fθ, which only makes sense if the loss is with respect to Dθ. The use of D here is inconsistent with the paper's earlier notation and could mislead a reader into thinking the loss is evaluated using the oracle denoiser. This is a minor slip in notation, but it breaks the connection between the abstract loss form and the practical training objective.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This manuscript presents a rigorous and timely re-examination of diffusion model design, effectively addressing a critical gap in the literature by disentangling theoretical derivations from practical engineering choices to expose a clear, modular design space. The writing is exceptionally clear and well-structured, providing a compelling motivation for separating sampling dynamics, noise schedules, and network preconditioning, which facilitates reproducibility and targeted innovation. The contributions are well-motivated, with promising claims of achieving state-of-the-art fidelity while drastically reducing sampling steps to just 35 evaluations, a feat that could significantly lower inference costs. However, the most significant issue concerns the robustness and fairness of the empirical validation: while the efficiency gains are impressive, the paper must ensure that all comparisons are conducted under strictly equal computational budgets and FLOP counts, as speedups can sometimes be misleading if hidden costs in training stability or network capacity are not accounted for. Additionally, the authors' decision to deviate from theoretically natural schedules (e.g., $\sigma \propto \sqrt{t}$) requires strong justification beyond empirical metrics to demonstrate that these heuristics generalize across diverse data distributions and do not inadvertently induce artifacts or mode collapse, a known risk when aggressively reducing sampling complexity.

comments.title: Overstatement on computational cost dominance
comments.quote: Given that the sampling process is dominated entirely by the cost of Dθ, improvements in NFE translate directly to sampling speed.
comments.explanation: While evaluating the denoiser D_θ is by far the largest computational bottleneck in diffusion sampling, stating it is 'dominated entirely' is an absolute claim that overlooks non-negligible overheads such as tensor shape resizing (e.g., upscaling latent features), memory bandwidth limitations, and sampler bookkeeping. This is a minor framing issue that does not invalidate the core argument (NFE is a valid proxy for speed), but tempering it to 'dominated almost entirely' or 'constitutes the vast majority of the cost' would be more precise.

comments.title: Potential ambiguity in ODE simplification and scaling factor
comments.quote: With this choice, the ODE of Eq. 4 simplifies to d **x** / d _t_ = ( **x** − D( **x** ; t ) ) / t and σ and t become interchangeable.
comments.explanation: Readers familiar with standard probability flow ODE derivations (e.g., Song et al. 2021) expect the score-based ODE to scale with ˙σ/σ^2, which for σ(t)=t would yield (x-D)/t^2. The text's claim of a 1/t dependence is mathematically consistent with their specific Eq. 4 formulation and preconditioning choices, but the sudden appearance of 1/t instead of 1/t^2 may cause confusion without seeing the intermediate steps or Eq. 4 itself. While likely correct within their self-consistent notation, explicitly noting why the σ^2 in the score function cancels or transforms in this particular time parameterization would prevent potential misunderstandings for readers reproducing the derivation.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency for $S_{\text{noise}}$ scaling factor
comments.quote: Finally, we have found that the loss of detail can be partially counteracted by setting _S_ noise slightly above 1 to inflate the standard deviation for the newly added noise.
comments.explanation: Initially, the phrase 'setting $S_{\text{noise}}$ slightly above 1 to inflate the standard deviation' caught my attention because the provided context defines $S_{\text{noise}}$ as the 'Standard deviation for injected noise'. If it were already an absolute standard deviation, referencing a value 'above 1' without units or a baseline schedule would be physically meaningless. I checked standard diffusion sampling literature and the surrounding heuristic description, which indicates $S_{\text{noise}}$ actually functions as a dimensionless scaling multiplier for a base noise schedule. However, the passage never explicitly states what baseline or units this multiplier scales. This remains a minor notation inconsistency that could mislead a reproducer into treating $S_{\text{noise}}$ as an absolute std dev rather than a relative factor, potentially causing implementation errors when combined with different noise schedules.

comments.title: Conflation of time steps and noise levels in stochastic window definition
comments.quote: We address the drift toward oversaturated colors by only enabling stochasticity within a specific range of noise levels _ti ∈_ [ _S_ tmin _, S_ tmax].
comments.explanation: The text explicitly calls $t_i$ 'noise levels'. I checked the context and confirmed that the authors strongly advocate for the schedule $\sigma(t)=t$, which makes time and noise mathematically interchangeable. However, this schedule is formally introduced and justified a few pages later ('With this choice, the ODE... simplifies... and $\sigma$ and $t$ become interchangeable'). In this specific methodological sentence, invoking $t_i$ to define a noise-level window without restating the schedule assumption is notationally imprecise. A careful reader adapting this sampler to alternative standard schedules (e.g., $\sigma(t) \propto \sqrt{t}$ for VP/VE) might incorrectly apply the $[S_{\text{tmin}}, S_{\text{max}}]$ constraint to time steps rather than actual noise levels, leading to incorrect sampling behavior. Explicitly referring to $\sigma_i$ or restating the schedule dependency here would prevent this ambiguity.

comments.title: Grammatical typo in augmentation conditioning sentence
comments.quote: during inference we set the them to zero to guarantee that only non-augmented images are generated.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'set the them' contains a redundant determiner and is a clear typographical error. It should read 'set them to zero'. While this does not impact the technical description or reproducibility of the method, it detracts from the professionalism of the prose and should be corrected.

comments.title: Slight imprecision in equation reference for loss weight derivation
comments.quote: Eq. 8 shows that training Fθ as preconditioned in Eq. 7 incurs an effective per-sample loss weight of λ(σ) c_out(σ)[2].
comments.explanation: In the full paper, Eq. 8 explicitly defines the loss weighting function λ(σ). The claim that the effective weight on the parameters of F_θ is λ(σ)c_out(σ)² actually follows from substituting the preconditioned denoiser architecture (Eq. 7) into the L2 denoising objective (typically Eq. 6). The text attributes the derivation of this effective weight directly to Eq. 8, which is slightly imprecise. The mathematical conclusion is sound (the network scaling introduces a c_out² factor that is neutralized by the choice of λ), but the reference would be more accurate if it pointed to the loss formulation rather than just the definition of λ.

comments.title: Scalar standard deviation for multivariate distribution
comments.quote: Let us denote the data distribution by _p_ data( _**x**_ ), with standard deviation _σ_ data
comments.explanation: In multivariate settings (such as image data), a probability distribution is formally characterized by a covariance matrix rather than a single scalar standard deviation. The authors clearly intend to refer to the per-dimension marginal standard deviation or assume an isotropic data scale, which is a very common and practical shorthand in the ML literature. While strictly a minor notational ambiguity, it does not contradict the rest of the framework, nor does it hinder reproduction or understanding of the subsequent ODE formulation.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This manuscript represents a significant contribution to the field by successfully bridging the gap between theoretical diffusion modeling and practical engineering constraints. The authors provide a clear and compelling narrative regarding the "convolution" of design choices, organizing the content into a logical framework that isolates specific algorithmic levers. The writing is technical yet accessible, successfully motivating a departure from strictly theoretical derivations toward empirical optimization. However, the section introducing the common framework (specifically the shift to the ODE formulation) is mathematically dense; a potential issue lies in ensuring that this rigorous statistical formalism does not obscure the practical, modular design space promised in the abstract, requiring careful exposition to maintain clarity for practitioners who may not specialize in stochastic differential equations.

comments.title: Hyphenation error in a technical term
comments.quote: apply a higherorder Runge–Kutta method
comments.explanation: The term "higher-order" typically requires a hyphen when used as an adjective to describe the method (e.g., "higher-order Runge-Kutta method"). The omission of the hyphen is a minor stylistic typo.

comments.title: Redundant axis label text in figure caption
comments.explanation: The caption lists the sequence of noise levels twice. While this may be an artifact of the PDF layout or OCR, it results in redundant text that does not add value to the reader's understanding of Figure 1.

comments.title: Contradictory description of tangent direction
comments.quote: The tangent of the solution trajectory therefore always points towards the denoiser output.
comments.explanation: The preceding text states that an infinitesimal forward step nudges the sample away from the data distribution. The derived ODE is given as $\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{x - D(x; t)}{t}$. Since $x$ is a noisy sample and $D$ is the denoiser output (clean data), the vector $x - D$ points *away* from the denoiser output (from Clean to Noisy). Therefore, the tangent vector ($\frac{dx}{dt}$) points away from the data. The text claiming it points 'towards' is logically inconsistent with the math and the earlier statement about the forward direction.

comments.title: Unclear/Corrupted schedule formula in Table 1
comments.explanation: The OCR-recovered formulas in the 'Schedule' columns (VP, VE, iDDPM) are heavily garbled. For example, 'e^(β_d t^2+β_min t−^1)' appears to be a corrupted version of $\exp(-\beta_d t^2 + ...)$, and '$√t t$' is indecipherable. Without the correct notation, the reader cannot verify the mathematical correctness of the proposed schedules compared to the original referenced works (VP, VE). While assuming plausible symbols usually resolves this, the specific expressions here are ambiguous enough that the reader cannot confirm they match standard definitions or the authors' own claims.
